a: Authentication is the system checking that you really are who you say you are, usually by asking for a password, a code, or a fingerprint. It answers the question "are you really this person?" before letting you in.
fs: Authentication verifies identity (who you are); factors split into something you know, have, or are.
fj: Mixing up authentication with authorization, thinking logging in also decides what you're allowed to do.

a: Authorization decides what an already-logged-in user is allowed to do or see, like whether you can delete a file or only read it. It happens after authentication, once the system already knows who you are.
fs: Authorization controls permissions (what you can do) and is enforced per action after identity is established.
fj: Assuming that once a user is authenticated they should be allowed to do anything, so they skip permission checks.

a: Encryption scrambles data using a key so that anyone without the key just sees gibberish, and only someone with the right key can unscramble it back. It protects information while it's stored or sent across a network.
fs: Encryption is reversible scrambling with a key; the same or a paired key turns ciphertext back into plaintext.
fj: Confusing encryption with hashing and expecting to "un-hash" a value, but hashing is one-way and can't be reversed.

a: Hashing runs data through a one-way function that always produces the same fixed-size fingerprint, and you cannot turn that fingerprint back into the original. Passwords are stored as hashes so a stolen database doesn't reveal the real passwords.
fs: Hashing is a deterministic one-way fingerprint; verify by re-hashing input and comparing, never by reversing.
fj: Thinking a hash can be decrypted back to the original, or that hashing is the same as encryption.

a: The CIA triad is the three goals security tries to protect: Confidentiality (only the right people see data), Integrity (data isn't secretly changed), and Availability (the system stays up and usable). Most security decisions trace back to protecting one of these three.
fs: The CIA triad frames security as Confidentiality, Integrity, and Availability; every control supports at least one.
fj: Thinking security is only about secrecy (Confidentiality) and forgetting Integrity and Availability matter too.
